coredata.dc:title: Luminescent composite material fabricated from carbon dots and red phosphors for application to UV-LED chips and metal ion detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Jung, Jae Yong
Park, Jin Young
Hong, Woo Tae
Kim, Young Kuk
Yang, Hyun Kyoung
coredata.dc:description:
Carbon dots with a size of 5 nm were synthesized through hydrothermal synthesis by recycling discarded coffee powder. In the Raman signal, the typical D (1364 cm−1) and G (1563 cm−1) bands of Sp
2 carbon were detected, and when excited at 324 nm, a blue, wide-range emission spectrum signal was detected. The synthesized carbon dots were mixed with a solution containing 10 ppm of Pb, Cr, and Cd to be used in the field of detecting metal ions in a solution state. Carbon dots that reacted with metal ions lost their unique blue luminescent color and showed that they could be used as metal ion detection sensors. In addition, spherical particle CaWO4:Eu3+ phosphor powder was synthesized and prepared by coprecipitation at room temperature. The synthesized phosphor had a tetragonal structure, and a strong signal from the (112) plane, the main peak, was detected in the XRD pattern, showing excellent crystallinity. When excited at 267 nm, a strong red emission signal of 613 nm was detected, and when the synthesized carbon dots were impregnated and made into a composite, an emission spectrum was observed in a wide range from blue to red, showing white color. This composite was mixed with PDMS to make a flexible sheet and applied to a UV-LED chip, suggesting that it could be used as a white light-emitting device.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
CaWO4
Composite
Coffee ground waste
Phosphors

Fabricated white emitting composite and flexible sheet for LED device
Heavy metal ions detection used carbon dots solution
Characteristics of CaWO4:Eu3+ phosphors and CaWO4:Eu3+@CDs composite
Proposal for application of CaWO4:Eu3+@CDs composite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental procedure; (a) Synthesis of carbon dots from wasted coffee powder and (b) Synthesis of CaWO4:Eu3+ red phosphors by co-precipitation at room temperature. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman shift of synthesized carbon dots.
XPS spectra; (a) Full scan survey, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s.
(a) TEM image, (b) Particle size contribution, and (c) surface plaid profiling of carbon dots.
(a) PLE and PL spectrum and (b) Images of different light of colloidal carbon dots.
(a) XRD pattern, (b) particle sized calculated using Scherrer's equation, (c) Revelated refinement, and (d) Schematic of tetragonal structure of CaWO4:Eu3+.
(a) Raman shift, (b) XPS full scan survey, (c) Ca 2p, (d), W 4f, (e) O 1s, and (f) Eu 3d of CaWO4:Eu3+.
(a) FE-SEM image and (b) EDS mapping of CaWO4:Eu3+.
(a) UV–Vis spectrum transferred bandgap energy (inset) and (b) PLE and PL spectrum CaWO4:Eu3+.
Raman shift of CaWO4:Eu3+ and CaWO4:Eu3+ .@CDs.
(a) PL spectra of CaWO4:Eu3+.@CDs under 267 nm, (b) Change of PL spectrum intensity according to CDs concentration, and (c) CIE coordinate.
(a) Photograph of UV-LED chip on CaWO4:Eu3+ and CaWO4:Eu3+.@CDs, (b) Flexible sheet, (c) Metal ions detected of colloidal carbon dots.
Analysis element of wasted coffee grounds.

row.entry: Analysis element of wasted coffee grounds

head.ce:title: Luminescent composite material fabricated from carbon dots and red phosphors for application to UV-LED chips and metal ion detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Marine-Bionics Convergence Technology Center, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Marine-Bionics convergence technology center, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical, Electronics and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical, Electronics and Software Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical, Electronics and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Powder & Ceramics Division, Korea Institute of Materials Science, 797 Changwondaero, Changwon, 51508, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Powder & Ceramics Division, Korea Institute of Materials Science, 797 Changwondaero, Changwon, 51508, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Marine-Bionics Convergence Technology Center, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Carbon dots with a size of 5 nm were synthesized through hydrothermal synthesis by recycling discarded coffee powder. In the Raman signal, the typical D (1364 cm−1) and G (1563 cm−1) bands of Sp
2 carbon were detected, and when excited at 324 nm, a blue, wide-range emission spectrum signal was detected. The synthesized carbon dots were mixed with a solution containing 10 ppm of Pb, Cr, and Cd to be used in the field of detecting metal ions in a solution state. Carbon dots that reacted with metal ions lost their unique blue luminescent color and showed that they could be used as metal ion detection sensors. In addition, spherical particle CaWO4:Eu3+ phosphor powder was synthesized and prepared by coprecipitation at room temperature. The synthesized phosphor had a tetragonal structure, and a strong signal from the (112) plane, the main peak, was detected in the XRD pattern, showing excellent crystallinity. When excited at 267 nm, a strong red emission signal of 613 nm was detected, and when the synthesized carbon dots were impregnated and made into a composite, an emission spectrum was observed in a wide range from blue to red, showing white color. This composite was mixed with PDMS to make a flexible sheet and applied to a UV-LED chip, suggesting that it could be used as a white light-emitting device.

ce:section.ce:para: Research is currently underway on light-emitting compounds doped with rare-earth ions aimed at achieving high-quality white light for their application in light conversion and display technologies. In the fields of displays and lighting, there is a growing demand for materials that can efficiently produce the three primary colors: red, green, and blue (RGB), while maintaining high color purity. Blue or ultraviolet light-emitting diodes (UV-LEDs) are often used for this purpose [1,2]. Depending on their availability, yellow or RGB luminescent materials can be used for light conversion [3]. Although white light is commonly generated by combining YAG:Ce3+ yellow phosphor powder with a blue LED, the color-rendering index of this system is lower than that of natural light [4,5]. Red or green phosphors are typically added [6–9]. To improve the color rendering index, significant efforts have been made to develop new high-efficiency RGB phosphors [10,11]. For example, Lovisa et al. studied the effect of charge compensation on the emission intensity of the CaWO4:Eu3+ Tm3+ red phosphor, which has a peak emission at 475–725 nm [12], whereas Zhang et al. prepared Eu2+-doped KBaPO4 and NaSrPO4 with a β-K2SO4-type structured phosphor and a blue emission at 420 nm [13]. Yi et al. achieved white-light emission by co-doping Dy3+ and Sm3+ ions in BaMoO4 as the host matrix at room temperature using a coprecipitation method [14]. Yang et al. synthesized a Ba3Bi(PO4):Sm3+ orange phosphor with a peak at 599 nm by sintering at 1250 °C for 4 h via a solid-state reaction method [15]. Gupta et al. utilized Gd2Si2O7 as a host to explore changes in structure and light-emitting characteristics with varying amounts of added Tb3+ ions and applied it to create a green light-emitting device using an LED chip [16]. Jung et al. produced a CaWO4:Dy3+ phosphor doped with Dy3+ ions in a CaWO4 host matrix by coprecipitation at room temperature. The synthesized phosphor emitted both blue light from the host and yellow light from the Dy3+ ions, resulting in a white emission spectrum [17]. As in previously reported cases, the emission wavelength and color can vary depending on the type of rare earth ions doped into the host of the inorganic compound. Generally, rare earth ions possess multiple energy bands in the visible light range, making them suitable as highly efficient luminescent materials for RGB colors. However, their collection often poses environmental challenges because of the need to extract these ions from nature. Wang et al. prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots through hydrothermal synthesis and observed changes in carbon dot properties depending on various concentrations of metal ions such as Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+, suggesting that they can be used as a wide range of sensors [18]. Additionally, Boobalan et al. reported that when carbon dots extracted from mushrooms reacted with Pb2+ ions of 58.63 μM, the luminescent properties disappeared, making detection possible [19].

In this study, to reduce the reliance on rare earth ions and produce an environmentally friendly high color-rendering index white-light material, Eu3+ ions were incorporated into blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) derived from discarded coffee grounds and CaWO4 host crystals known for their chemical stability. These components were used to create a composite that emits white light when applied to a flexible material and paired with a UV-LED chip. A CD solution was used to detect the heavy metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from coffee ground waste
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized from coffee ground waste precursors via hydrothermal treatment. Nitric acid (70%, Ducksan chemical) and iron nitrate (Fe(NO3)3··9H2O, 99.9%, Aldrich) were used as an oxidizing agent and catalyst, respectively. Coffee ground waste (1 g) iron nitrate (0.76 g), and nitric acid (1 mL) were added to 40 mL of deionized water and stirred vigorously for 10 min. The solution was transferred to a Teflon vessel and placed in a stainless-steel autoclave. Hydrothermal processing was performed at 230 °C for 20 h. Subsequently, the solution changed from transparent to yellow because of the formation of CDs. The yellowish solution was purified by membrane filtration. The purified solution was dialyzed against deionized water, as shown in Fig. 1 (a) [20].

Calcium nitrate (1 mmol) was dissolved in beaker ‘A’ containing 50 mL of deionized water. In beaker ‘B,’ 1 mmol sodium tungstate (Na2WO4) was added under the same conditions (Fig. 1(b)). The beaker ‘B’ solution completely dissolved and was slowly poured into beaker ‘A,’ which was stirred continuously and incubated for approximately 60 min. Subsequently, the white powder was recovered using a centrifuge (4000 rpm, 10 min) and prepared by rinsing with distilled water twice to remove the remaining unreacted agent. The white powder was then dried in an oven at 80 °C for 20 h to investigate its crystallinity and luminescence. In addition, 0.025 mmol europium nitrate (Eu(NO3)3·xH2O) ions were added to beaker ‘A’ during coprecipitation to impart luminescent properties, as shown in Fig. 1(b) [21–25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabricated white emitting composite and flexible sheet for LED device
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CD solution was transferred to a beaker, stirred, and 0.1 g of red-emitting CaWO4:Eu3+ phosphor powder was introduced to the beaker. After stirring for 30 min, the beaker was placed in a dryer, vacuum was applied, and the mixture was maintained at 80 °C for 5 h to prepare a composite. The effect of impregnating carbon dots into the pores formed on the CaWO4:Eu3+ surface was induced. The prepared composite powder was mixed with polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) to form a flexible sheet and applied to a UV-LED chip (A product manufactured in China with a central emission wavelength of 270 nm was purchased., Cree LED, SGH company, China). The flexible sheet exhibited white luminescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heavy metal ions detection used carbon dots solution
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the changes in luminescence characteristics due to reactions with heavy metal ions using the synthesized CDs, an aqueous solution with a heavy metal ion (Pb, Cr, or Cd) concentration of 10 ppm was prepared. After adding 1 ml of a heavy metal aqueous solution to 5 mL of carbon quantum dots, a 303 nm laser was irradiated to photograph the change in luminescence characteristics.

The carbon content of the coffee powder waste was determined using an elemental analyzer (TRUSPEC MICRO/CNS928, LECO). Structural characterization by X-ray diffraction (XRD, PANalytical/X'pert 3-powder) was performed with Cu Kα radiation (
λ =
1.5406 Å). The chemical compositions of the samples were studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; KRATOS Analytical Ltd./AXIS SUPRA) using Al-Kα lines. The C1s peak (284.6 eV) was used to calibrate the peak positions of the insulating samples. The surface morphology was investigated using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; SU-8220, Hitachi, Japan) and field-emission transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM; JEOL, JEM-2100F). The absorbances were recorded on an ultraviolet–visible (UV/Vis; Shimadzu/UV 18000) spectrometer. The Raman shifts were observed using a Raman spectrometer (LabRam-HR 800, Horiba Jobin-Yvon). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (JASCO, V-670) with a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source and a photomultiplier tube operating at 200 V. Origin Pro 2018 software was used for data fitting and visualization.

Raman spectroscopy also confirmed the structural properties of the CDs, and the spectrum exhibits two peaks at 1364.24 cm−1 and 1563.23 cm−1 (Fig. 2(b)), corresponding to the D and G bands of graphitic carbon, respectively. The ratio of ID/IG is 1.74, which is characteristic of the disordered amorphous nature and the ratio of Sp
3 /Sp
2 carbon, implying structural defects in the synthesized CDs. Aschalew et al. synthesized CDs through hydrothermal synthesis using lemons and detected the Raman signals of the D and G bands at 1348 cm−1 and 1547 cm−1, respectively. The intensity of the G band signal was approximately 1.03 times that of the D band, which is consistent with the results of our previous study [27].

XPS was performed to determine the chemical bond energy states of the synthesized CDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3(a) shows that the full-scan survey spectrum, and peak signals were detected at the 1s positions of O, N, and C. For detailed observation, high-resolution detection of each element was performed. The Gaussian-fitted C 1s spectra revealed the formation of Sp
2 carbon (C–C and CC; 284.9 eV), carbon–nitrogen (C–N and C–N; 286.2 eV), carbonyl (CO; 286.8 eV), and carboxylate (O–CO; 288.1 eV) binding on the surface of the CDs (Fig. 3(b)). Pyridinic (399.7 eV) and graphitic (401.4 eV) nitrogen can be observed (Fig. 3(c)), suggesting the existence of substituted nitrogen in the CDs. The O1s spectra were fitted to the sum of three Gaussian peaks, which correspond to the functionalized group (528.9 eV) and single (531.4 eV) and double bonds (532.4 eV) with aromatic carbon (Fig. 3(d)) [28].

To determine the size and morphology of the CDs synthesized by hydrothermal treatment, FE-TEM imaging was performed, as shown in Fig. 4 . In the high-resolution TEM image of the CDs in Fig. 4(a), the synthesized CDs exhibit a round and graphitic carbon structure. The size of the nanoparticles was approximately 5.5 nm and a crystal lattice pattern appears on their surface (Fig. 4(b)). The interplanar spacing between the crystal lattices was found to be 0.199 nm (Fig. 4(c)).

The PL excitation (PLE) and PL spectra of the CD nanoparticles synthesized using the hydrothermal method are shown in Fig. 5 . The CDs exhibit strong absorption over a wide spectrum in the ultraviolet (UV) range of 230–350 nm. When the nanoparticles were excited at 324 nm, the central peak of the PLE spectrum exhibited a visible light emission spectrum with a wide range (350–600 nm) and a central peak at 395 nm (Fig. 5(a)). Xue et al. carbonized lychee seeds by heat treatment at 300 °C and powdered them. The carbonized powder was placed in deionized water and subjected to ultrasonication to produce the CDs. When the fabricated CDs were excited at 365 nm, a blue emission spectrum with a peak at 440 nm was observed, which was similar to previously reported results [29]. The synthesized CD solution appeared as a yellow transparent solution under ordinary light, but was blue under a UV lamp (Fig. 5(b)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of CaWO4:Eu3+ phosphors and CaWO4:Eu3+@CDs composite
ce:section.ce:para:
Crystalline CaWO4:Eu3+ was synthesized at room temperature via coprecipitation. The chemical reactions proceeded as follows [30]:
(1)

The aquation solution when beaker ‘B’ is poured into beaker ‘A’:

c o p r e c i p i t a t i o n

O

Finally, CaWO4:Eu3+ was synthesized as a white powder by coprecipitation. XRD analysis was performed to determine the structural characteristics, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 (a). In the XRD pattern of the synthesized CaWO4:Eu3+, peaks were observed at the signal positions of the (101), (112), (004), (200), (211), (204), and (312) planes, and the main peak appeared at (112). A strong signal on the plane was detected and was consistent with the reference data (ICDD card No. 01-085-0433) and the tetragonal structure (scheelite, space group: I41/a). The particle size of the synthesized crystalline CaWO4:Eu3+ was calculated from the XRD patterns using the Scherrer equation [31]:
(4) D = kλ/β
D
cosθ

Here, D is the particle size in nm, λ is the wavelength of radiation (Cu-Kα radiation, λ = 1.5406 Å), k is a constant equal to 0.94, β
D is the peak width at half-maximum, and θ is the peak position. The instrument-corrected position (β
D ) corresponding to the XRD diffraction peak of CaWO4:Eu3+ was estimated from the following equations:
(5)
β D 2
=
[
(
β 2
)
m e a s u r e d
−
(
β 2
)
i n s t r u m e n t a l
]
(6)
D
=
k λ
/
β D
cos
θ
→
cos
θ
k λ
/ D
(
1 /
β D
)

Cosθ versus
1 /
β D
plots were constructed using the XRD pattern, such that the fitted peak position for CaWO4:Eu3+, which has a tetragonal structure, appeared between 2θ values of 10–70°. The CaWO4:Eu3+ particle size D was 415 nm, which was extracted from the slope of the fitted line (Fig. 6(b)). Rietveld refinement was performed on the obtained diffraction data using the GASS II software result (Fig. 6(c)) [32]. The peaks and intensities of the experimental XRD pattern (Y obs) were consistent with the refinement data (Y cal), and the Y obs–Y cal values were very small (R wp = 4.27%, R p = 3.67%, X 2 = 6.431). The distances between the a, b length, and c-axis in the crystal structure of the synthesized CaWO4:Eu3+ were shown in Fig. 6(d), the lengths of a and b were same, and the structure was tetragonal with a long c-axis (a = b = 5.246 Å, c-axis: 11.381 Å, α = β = γ = 90°). Comparing the XRD peak of the (112) plane, which is the main peak of pure CaWO4 synthesized without a doped material and CaWO4:Eu3+ sample synthesized by doping with Eu3+ ions, a shift in the peak was observed as shown in Fig. S1. This is the result of doping of rare earth ions with a relatively large ionic radius. Calculating the lattice constant using the position of the main peak, CaWO4 showed a change of 3.10 Å and CaWO4:Eu3+ showed a change of 3.15 Å.

The Raman shift spectrum of the synthesized tetragonal crystalline CaWO4:Eu3+ is shown in Fig. 7 (a). Signals of the positions of B g (114, 204, and 834 cm−1), B g/A g (327 cm−1), E g (395, 793 cm−1), and A g (909 cm−1) were detected between 100 and 1000 cm−1. The peaks at 793 cm−1, 834 cm−1, and 909 cm−1 were ascribed to the (O–W–O)/(O–W–O) symmetric stretching vibration [31]. Peaks between 85 and 300 cm−1 were present due to the internal vibrational modes of the crystalline lattice, and are caused by the polarization of the WO4 −2 clusters in the unit cell. The peaks at 327 cm−1 and 395 cm−1 correspond to the vibrational modes of the W–O bonds [33].

In the Ca 2p XPS spectrum of CaWO4:Eu3+, peaks were observed at binding energies of 350.39 eV (2p1/2) and 346.88 eV (2p3/2), suggesting that Ca was present in the +2 oxidation state (Fig. 7(c)) [34]. In the W 4f spectrum, peaks were observed at binding energies of 37.21 eV (4f5/2) and 35.08 eV (4f7/2), corresponding to the +6 state of W (Fig. 7(d)) [34]. Peaks in the O 1s spectrum had binding energies of 531.78 eV and 530.36 eV, indicating the presence of oxygen in the CaWO4:Eu3+ crystal lattice (Fig. 7(e)). In addition, the peaks at binding energies corresponding to Eu3+ ions (1164.26 and 1154. 31 eV (3d3/2) and 1134.58 and 1124.88 eV (3d5/2)) were shown in Fig. 7(f).

FE-SEM was performed to determine the surface shape and size of the synthesized CaWO4:Eu3+ particles, and component analysis was performed using EDS mapping, (Fig. 8 ). The spherical particles of approximately 4 μm in size had numerous pores on the surface (Fig. 8(a)), and Ca, W, O, and Eu were detected (Fig. 8(b)). The Ca2+ and WO4 complex ions in the aqueous solution acted as nuclei, controlling the kinetics of nucleation and growth during CaWO4 crystallization. During this process, large particles continued to grow, whereas relatively smaller particles disappeared or underwent Ostwald ripening [35], which was attributed to the presence of large particles. After crystal growth in an aqueous medium, rotation and alignment to a common crystallographic plane occurred through self-assembly. This process was not controllable, and random and spontaneous aggregation of the crystal particles occurred, resulting in the formation of CaWO4:Eu3+ microspheres. The optical bandgap of the CaWO4:Eu3+ particles synthesized was examined using reflectance UV–Vis spectroscopy across a spectral range of 200–800 nm (Fig. 9 (a)). In a previous study [36], optical absorption was observed in the electronic transitions from the valence band O 2p orbitals to the conduction band W 5d orbitals. The primary electronic transitions in CaWO4 governing the excitation and recombination of electrons within the bands are driven by directly allowed transitions with n = 0.5 [34]. The optical bandgap (E bandgap) of CaWO4:Eu3+ was determined using a Tauc plot using the following equation [37]:
(8)
(
α h ν
)
1 / n
= X
(
h ν
−
E
b a n d g a p
)
Here, α is the linear absorption coefficient, hν the photon energy, and X is a proportionality constant. The calculated bandgap of the synthesized CaWO4:Eu3+ was calculated to be 4.47 eV.

The CaWO4:Eu3+ PLE spectrum, obtained at a photoluminescence (PL) wavelength of 613 nm, exhibited a peak at 267 nm and was broadly distributed over a range from 240 to 480 nm (Fig. 9(b)). This range corresponds to the absorption spectrum resulting from the charge transfer bands generated between the O−2-W6+ and O−2-Eu3+ ions [38]. The relatively weak absorption signals observed between 310 and 380 nm correspond to the 4f–4f transition signals of the Eu3+ ions [36]. The peak signals observed at 319, 362, 382, 394, 416, and 464 nm are attributed to the 7F0 → 5H3, 7F0 → 5D4, 7F0 → 5G2, 5G3, 7F0 → 5L6, 7F0 → 5D3, and 7F0 → 5D2 transition signals, respectively. Upon excitation at the most prominent absorption wavelength of 267 nm, the PL spectra of CaWO4:Eu3+ exhibit emissions at 591 (5D0 → 7F1), 613 (5D0 → 7F2), 649 (5D0 → 7F3), and 693 (5D0 → 7F4) nm. Of these, the signal at 613 nm represents an electric dipole transition, which at 591 nm corresponds to a magnetic dipole transition, and those at 650 and 700 nm signify electric dipole signals [39]. Notably, the PL intensity at 613 nm was approximately five times stronger than that at 591 nm, indicating that the Eu3+ ions in the host lattice occupied non-inversion-symmetric sites. The ratio between the intensities of red and orange emissions is often referred to as the asymmetry ratio [39]. To produce a white-light-emitting material, a composite of CDs with a blue light-emitting spectrum and CaWO4:Eu3+ with a red light-emitting spectrum was used. Raman shift spectra were obtained to confirm that the CDs were applied to CaWO4:Eu3+ (Fig. 10 ). The spectra of pure CaWO4:Eu3+ and CaWO4:Eu3+ with CDs (CaWO4:Eu3+@CDs) were identical between 100 and 1000 cm−1, which can be attributed to the presence of CaWO4. However, between 1350 and 1700 cm−1, no signal was detected for the pure CaWO4:Eu3+ sample, and signals in the D and G bands were detected in the CaWO4:Eu3+@CDs sample, confirming that CDs were applied. Fig. 11 shows the change in the PL emission characteristics of the composite fabricated by varying the amount of CD solution in the CaWO4:Eu3+ red-emitting material. The concentration of CDs in the solution was 0.21 mg/ml. When the pure red-emitting CaWO4:Eu3+ material was excited at 267 nm, only the spectrum corresponding to the Eu3+ ions was observed. However, as the number of blue-emitting CDs increased, the intensity of the emission spectrum increased over a wide range from 360 to 560 nm (Fig. 11(a) and (b)). The synthesized CDs are located in the blue region according to the Commission Internationale de l’Éclairage system (0.134, 0.168), and pure CaWO4:Eu3+ is located in the red region (0.643, 0.341). The samples made of the composites moved to the white area (Fig. 11(c)). In general, white-light emission is achieved by co-doping two types of rare-earth ions, such as Tb3+/Eu3+ [39] and Dy3+/Sm3+ [40]. However, in this study, a composite comprising CDs synthesized from recycled coffee grounds and a CaWO4:Eu3+ luminescent material at room temperature to reduce energy consumption realized white-light emission, and a process with eco-friendly elements was proposed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposal for application of CaWO4:Eu3+@CDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CaWO4:Eu3+ and CaWO4:Eu3+@CDs powders were mixed with PDMS, cast onto a UV-LED chip, poured into a round mold, and cured. The UV-LED chip emitted red and white light when a voltage of approximately 6V was applied (Fig. 12 (a)). The sheet, which was round, was white under ordinary light and had a unique emission of red and white when irradiated with a UV lamp (Fig. 12(b)). The manufactured sheet was sufficiently flexible to be bent easily with the fingers. Fig. 12(c) shows a photograph of an aqueous solution containing 10 ppm of dissolved heavy metal ions added to a CD solution and irradiated with a 303 nm portable diode laser. Aqueous solutions containing dissolved heavy metal ions such as Pb, Cr, and Cd reacted with the CDs and lost their luminescent properties. The light emission of carbon quantum dots has been reported to disappear as a result of the photoinduced electron transfer of electrons excited in the carbon quantum dots by metal ions [41]. Rather than absorbing excitation light and being excited and transitioning to the ground level to emit light, the electrons in the carbon quantum dot are attenuated as the excited electrons move to the metal ion through the intermolecular deactivation process, and the ground level of the carbon quantum dot is attenuated. As holes are formed, the excited electrons transition to ground level and the luminescence characteristics decrease or disappear [42]. Samples fabricated from the synthesized CDs, red phosphors, and composites have the potential for application in LEDs, flexible sheets, and heavy metal ion detection.

CDs and nanoparticles were synthesized using a hydrothermal method involving recycled coffee grounds. The synthesized CDs exhibited the characteristic turbostratic structure of the (002) plane in the XRD pattern and unique signals corresponding to the D and G bands in the Raman shift. The chemical bonding energy, as determined by XPS, indicated the presence of Sp
2 carbon and C–O signals, and revealed that the particles had a spherical shape with a diameter of approximately 5 nm. The CD nanoparticles exhibited a blue emission spectrum covering a wide range, from 400 to 600 nm, when excited at 324 nm. To prepare the red-emitting phosphor, CaWO4:Eu3+ was synthesized at room temperature via coprecipitation. The synthesized sample exhibited a tetragonal structure, and the XRD signal of the (112) plane, that is, the primary peak, was detected at a high intensity. A unique symmetric stretching signal of tungstate was detected at high intensity in the Raman shift, and the XPS results exhibited characteristic peaks for Ca 2p, W 4f, O 1s, and Eu 3d. CaWO4:Eu3+, which has spherical particles measuring approximately 4 μm, emitted the distinctive red spectrum of Eu3+ when excited at 267 nm. The prepared CD nanoparticles were impregnated with red-emitting CaWO4:Eu3+ powder to create a white-emitting composite. With an increase in the volume of added CD solution, the intensity of the blue emission increased and shifted towards the white position in the color coordinates. The resulting composite was mixed with PDMS, cast onto a UV-LED chip to emit red and white light, and fabricated into a round flexible sheet. When the CDs in the solution were mixed with solutions containing heavy metal ions such as Pb, Cr, and Cd, they lost their unique luminescent properties, enabling the easy detection of heavy metal ions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Always provides us with invaluable information on the broad spectrum of materials science and related areas. This time, I would like to disclose some practically important results of our recent work on synthesis of red luminescent Eu3+ doped CaWO4 via co-precipitation at room temperature and blue luminescent carbon dots from wasted coffee powder via hydrothermal. We also characterized structural and photoluminescence property of CaWO4:Eu3+ and carbon dots. The main objective of present work is to report a novel method of synthesizing Eu3+ doped CaWO4 and carbon dots via uniform chemical mixing of precursors and to provide insights to crystal structure and photoluminescence property relationship. Finally, we suggest the possibility of Eu3+ doped phosphors and carbon dots incapsulated CaWO4:Eu3+@ CDs composites using for flexible composite, UV-LED, metal ions detector applications. The work described in this manuscript has not been published previously that it is not under consideration for publication elsewhere and its publication is approved by all authors and tacitly or explicitly by the responsible authorities where the work was carried out. We agree that submission, if accepted, will not be published elsewhere in the same form, in English or in any other language, without the written consent of the Publisher. When you need further contact regarding the present manuscript, please forward a message to may address. Thank you in advance for your help.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) and funded by the Ministry of Education (grant numbers: RS-2023-00240874, RS-2023-00237313, and RS-2023-00246326).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceramint.2023.12.119.
